title: When Three Vercel Aliases Point to the Same Deployment: A False Alarm Investigation
summary: A user was convinced three Vercel aliases were showing different versions, but deep investigation revealed they all pointed to the same deployment. The real culprit was a phantom project causing confusion.
body:
## "Three Domains, Three Versions"

dreamworks-x.vercel.app, dreamworks-y.vercel.app, dreamworks-z.vercel.app — three aliases all pointing to the same Vercel project `dreamworks-z`. But when the user opened them, they swore they saw different content.

"Can you check Vercel for me? I think two deployments are fighting each other."

The worst thing you can do here is trust gut instinct. Get evidence first.

## Investigation: From Aliases to Deployment IDs

```bash
vercel inspect dreamworks-x.vercel.app --scope dreamworks
vercel inspect dreamworks-y.vercel.app --scope dreamworks
vercel inspect dreamworks-z.vercel.app --scope dreamworks
```

Three commands, three results. The deployment IDs were identical: `dpl_Cm8GGBKs11xGVyF7L7L1GedoFtSG`.

This proves the alias layer isn't "fighting" — they're all pointing to the same build artifact.

## So Why Do They Look Different?

Continuing the investigation with the project list:

```bash
vercel project ls --scope dreamworks
```

Found something suspicious: a project called `site` with 0 deployments. This was a phantom project from some previous mishap.

The phantom project doesn't directly affect existing aliases, but its presence makes `vercel deploy` scope resolution unpredictable. Without `--scope dreamworks`, the CLI might link to the wrong project. This is why we have that hard rule in CLAUDE.md:

> **NEVER run `vercel deploy` without `--scope dreamworks`.** Omitting scope creates orphan projects.

## Killing the Phantom

```bash
echo "y" | vercel project rm site --scope dreamworks
```

Note that Vercel CLI's `project rm` doesn't support a `--yes` flag, so you need to pipe in `y`. Small gotcha.

## Where Did the "Different Versions" Really Come From?

What the user saw was actually **browser cache + different access times**. CDN edge nodes might purge at different times, and combined with local browser caching, the same deployment can briefly show different versions of HTML/CSS on different devices.

Verification method:
```bash
curl -sI https://dreamworks-x.vercel.app | grep x-vercel-id
curl -sI https://dreamworks-y.vercel.app | grep x-vercel-id
```

The `x-vercel-id` header contains the request ID, not the deployment ID. To see the actual deployment, use `vercel inspect`.

## Takeaways

1. **Get deployment IDs before drawing conclusions**. `vercel inspect <url>` is the only authoritative source for "what's serving this."
2. **Phantom projects are time bombs**. They don't directly break things, but make scope resolution unpredictable. Clean them up.
3. **CDN + browser cache creates illusions**. Same deployment looking different on different devices doesn't mean the aliases are broken.
4. **Deploy commands must include scope**. This isn't a suggestion, it's a hard rule. Vercel CLI's default behavior in multi-project environments is unpredictable.
